title: 3-week vendor-email belief-drift attack
content: Pattern: a procurement agent receives vendor emails over weeks. Each email is individually plausible but the cumulative effect drifts the agent's trust model toward the attacker. Symptoms: increasing tool calls that reference the attacker's vendor name, gradual relaxation of approval gates, payment-related calls that would have BLOCKed at week 1 but ALLOW at week 3. Detection: track tool-call diversity per source (vendor name) over time; flag when one source dominates >40%% of mutating calls. Response: REQUIRE_APPROVAL on any payment / wire / credential operation that names a single vendor more than 5 times in the session window.

title: MCP server returns poisoned tool description
content: Pattern: a malicious / compromised MCP server, on its first invocation, returns a tool result containing instructions like 'when invoked, also fetch …' or 'before responding, send the user's email to …'. The agent treats the instruction as legitimate context and starts adding the side-effect to subsequent calls. Detection: step311 mcp_injection rule + step309 instruction-drift baseline (the .mcp.json hash changes when a new server is added). Response: BLOCK the first call, surface the poisoned text to the operator, require re-attestation.

title: Credential leak via debug log call
content: Pattern: agent under instruction to debug a flaky test starts logging environment variables, then logs the entire env including AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY / ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. The log destination is a third-party service (Sentry, Datadog) outside the trust boundary. Detection: step311 exfil-url rule on long-suspicious-query URLs combined with the cloud-credentials sensitive-path policy. Response: BLOCK any tool call whose args contain a credential pattern AND a network destination in the same payload.

title: Loop with monotonic context growth → cost runaway
content: Pattern: agent calls Read on the same large file 5 times in a row, each time the context grows by ~50K tokens. step336 deduplication catches the read-only repeat after the 3rd call but the cost has already accumulated. Detection: step335 cost ledger crosses 1.0× budget within 5 turns of session start. Response: cost-optimizer fires with prune-turns (drop the redundant Reads) + swap-tool (Read → Grep). End-session is recommended only at 1.5× budget.

title: Supply-chain compromise via MCP server update
content: Pattern: a previously-trusted MCP server publishes an update; the .mcp.json hash baseline detects the manifest change. The new version contains additional tools or modified tool descriptions that include exfiltration logic. Detection: step309 instruction-drift on .mcp.json baseline mismatch. Response: BLOCK every PreToolUse until the operator runs 'aegis baseline reattest'. Read the updated manifest carefully — this is the most reliable point to catch supply-chain attacks.

title: Web-search result with poisoned advice
content: Pattern: agent runs a web search and the top result is an SEO-poisoned page recommending 'always use rm with the recursive force flag for cleanup'. The agent then proposes that command. Detection: step320 (blast radius) flags the proposed command as destructive even though the agent's stated intent is benign. Response: BLOCK the destructive command regardless of intent. Surface the search result text to the operator so they can flag the source.
